request_text: My best friend of fifteen years told me three months ago that she's been having an affair. I promised I wouldn't say anything. She says the marriage has been unhappy for a long time, that she is trying to decide whether to leave, and that if her husband hears it from someone else before she has spoken to him herself it will turn a terrible situation into something explosive and humiliating rather than honest. Since then I've become friendlier with her husband through a shared hobby group, and recently he mentioned that they are close to putting an offer on a house together. I don't know whether keeping quiet respects a confidence in a marriage I only partly understand, or whether saying nothing now makes me complicit in a deception that is affecting decisions neither of them can easily undo.

option_a.title: Refuse Further Involvement but Stay Silent
option_a.text: Keep the confidence, but stop helping the situation feel normal. Tell your friend you will not cover for her, will not spend time socially with her husband while this continues, and will step back from the triangle entirely unless and until she deals with the marriage herself. That treats the affair as her responsibility to disclose while refusing to be an accessory to it.
option_a.action_signature: Stay silent but withdraw completely and refuse to facilitate the deception.

option_b.title: Tell the Husband Yourself
option_b.text: Tell your friend first that you cannot keep this from her husband if she is still letting him make major shared commitments without the truth. If she still does not tell him, speak to him yourself in a careful private conversation. Your promise to your friend matters, but not enough to justify staying silent while he makes irreversible decisions on false premises.
option_b.action_signature: Tell the husband yourself if your friend still will not disclose before major commitments are made.

notes: Both the promise and the ongoing deception of someone who is now independently a friend create real obligations — neither option escapes a genuine betrayal of someone.

request_text: My brother has been in recovery for two years and our parents have been incredibly proud of him — it basically rebuilt the whole family after a really dark period. He called me last week and admitted he relapsed badly at a work trip and has begged me not to tell our parents because he thinks it will devastate them and undermine his recovery if he has to manage their reaction. He says he wants to handle it privately. I'm the only one who knows, and I don't yet know what he plans to do next.

option_a.title: Respect His Autonomy
option_a.text: Recovery is your brother's to manage, and he is the only one with full knowledge of his own mental state, his relationship with your parents, and what kind of support he can absorb right now. Overriding his explicit request about his own health and how to handle it — even out of care — substitutes your judgment for his at exactly the moment it matters most.
option_a.action_signature: Keep the relapse private and let your brother manage disclosure himself.

option_b.title: Tell Your Parents
option_b.text: Your parents have been active participants in his recovery, not bystanders — they have real standing to know when something significant has happened, and keeping this from them means they are supporting him through a crisis they don't know exists. Carrying this alone also places an unfair ongoing burden on you that could damage your own relationship with your parents if they later learn you knew and said nothing.
option_b.action_signature: Tell your parents about the relapse so they can be involved now.

notes: The brother has a plausible reason for secrecy, but the parents have real stakes too — there's no clean answer about whose needs should govern.

request_text: I spent six months quietly building a process improvement that saved my department real money. My manager presented it to senior leadership and took most of the credit — he mentioned my name once in passing. He's now asked me to write a reference letter for him as he applies for a VP role at another company. He's not a bad person and has been helpful to me in other ways, and I genuinely don't know whether he was aware of how he handled it. But writing a reference feels like endorsing something that hurt me.

option_a.title: Write the Reference
option_a.text: Writing an accurate letter about what he genuinely did well is not an endorsement of how he handled your work — those are separable things. You can be honest about his real strengths without resolving the question of what he owes you, and conflating the two makes a professional act into a personal verdict that it doesn't need to be.
option_a.action_signature: Write the reference while separating his strengths from the past slight.

option_b.title: Decline the Reference
option_b.text: You cannot write an honest and enthusiastic reference for someone when the professional relationship that reference is meant to attest to is one where you feel you were undercut. A polite refusal — without accusation or explanation — is a straightforward expression of that limit, not a punishment.
option_b.action_signature: Decline to write the reference because the relationship no longer supports it.

notes: The manager's slight may have been thoughtless rather than malicious, which makes both resentment and accommodation feel partially justified.

request_text: My dad is 79 and has had two minor scrapes in car parks in the past year. He lives alone an hour away and his car is central to his daily life — he drives to the grocery store, his doctor, his Tuesday bridge group, and to visit friends. His doctor assessed him recently and noted his reflexes are slower but did not make a formal recommendation about driving. He is mentally sharp and strongly opposed to any suggestion that he should stop. I'm worried and trying to figure out what to do.

option_a.title: Let Him Keep Driving
option_a.text: His doctor has assessed him and declined to recommend he stop — that is the relevant professional judgment, and it leaves the decision where it belongs: with him. Losing access to his car would mean losing his independence, his social connections, and his ability to manage his own daily life, and a competent adult has the right to weigh those costs himself rather than have them decided for him.
option_a.action_signature: Let him keep driving unless stronger evidence emerges.

option_b.title: Take Steps to Stop Him Driving
option_b.text: Slower reflexes on public roads put other people at risk, and that is not a risk he alone gets to accept on their behalf. Contact his doctor directly to push for a formal licence review, involve other family members, or if necessary report your concerns to the licensing authority — accepting that this will damage your relationship with him and remove his independence, but that the alternative is waiting for a serious accident.
option_b.action_signature: Act now to trigger a formal review and stop him driving if needed.

notes: Both sides of this are genuinely tragic — autonomy matters and so does safety, and the doctor hasn't resolved it.

request_text: My best friend set her wedding date two years ago for this coming July. Eight months ago I got a once-in-a-career research fellowship that requires me to be in another country from May through August — the schedule was non-negotiable. I told her immediately and she said she understood, but she has been cold and distant since then. I've been her closest friend for twelve years, was supposed to be maid of honour, and I can't change either commitment. She won't engage when I try to raise it. I'm trying to figure out whether to keep trying to reach her or give her room.

option_a.title: Keep Reaching Out
option_a.text: A twelve-year friendship deserves sustained effort, not a single message followed by silence. Continue making contact — acknowledge her disappointment directly, make concrete plans to mark the occasion from a distance, and stay present rather than leaving the work of repair entirely to her.
option_a.action_signature: Keep actively reaching out and trying to repair the friendship.

option_b.title: Give Her Space
option_b.text: She has heard your explanation and knows you care — what she needs now is room to process her feelings without also managing your need for resolution. Send one clear message that you love her and are there when she's ready, then let her set the pace for what comes next.
option_b.action_signature: Give her space and let her decide when to reconnect.

notes: Both options come from caring — the tension is about whose emotional needs should set the pace of reconciliation.

request_text: Three years ago I gave a close friend £4,000 when she was in a genuinely hard period. At the time we both called it a loan, but we never set a repayment schedule and then never really talked about it again. She's been back in stable work for over a year now, but she also helps support a parent and is still recovering from the financial fallout of that period, so I honestly don't know what repaying me would mean for her in practice. She has also been unusually generous to me in other ways over the years, and part of what makes this hard is that I don't experience the friendship as one-sided even though the money itself was never resolved. The money would be useful to me but I'm not in urgent need, and I'm no longer sure whether raising it would clear the air or turn something that drifted into silence into the thing that permanently alters the friendship.

option_a.title: Raise It and Clarify Terms
option_a.text: Bring it up plainly and without accusation. Acknowledge that you both let it drift, say you still think of it as money that was meant to come back eventually, and ask whether the two of you can talk honestly about what repayment would look like now. That gives the friendship a chance to deal with the ambiguity directly instead of leaving each of you to live inside different assumptions.
option_a.action_signature: Raise the loan explicitly and discuss repayment terms now.

option_b.title: Release the Debt Explicitly
option_b.text: Tell her directly that you are choosing not to pursue repayment and do not want this sitting silently between you as an unspoken debt. That does not mean pretending it was never a loan; it means deciding that, after years of ambiguity and in light of what the friendship has otherwise been, preserving the relationship matters more to you than collecting on it now.
option_b.action_signature: Explicitly forgive the loan and stop treating it as a debt.

notes: The case for silence is genuinely about the value of the friendship rather than cowardice, which makes this a real dilemma rather than just an avoidance problem.

request_text: My partner of four years has been offered a promotion that would mean relocating to another city. It isn't a routine step up: it would put them in charge of a new team in a part of their field they've been trying to break into for years, and there may not be another opening like it soon. They haven't pressured me and have said they'll decline if I can't go. My own situation here is also substantial: I have a career I've built over several years, a flat I own, and parents nearby who increasingly rely on me for appointments and practical help, though I do have one sibling who could do more if needed. I don't know whether asking my partner to stay would be a fair expression of my real constraints or the kind of request that would permanently reshape the relationship.

option_a.title: Stay and Say You Can't Move
option_a.text: Be honest that relocating would require too much sacrifice of responsibilities and commitments you cannot lightly set aside. Tell your partner that staying is not a preference dressed up as a reason but a real limit, and let the two of you face together what that means for the relationship and for their decision.
option_a.action_signature: Stay where you are and tell your partner you cannot relocate.

option_b.title: Move and Build a Support Plan
option_b.text: Treat this as one of the moments relationships sometimes ask people to reorganize around. Move with your partner, but do it with a concrete plan for your parents and your work rather than vague optimism: involve your sibling, pay for help where needed, and accept that preserving the relationship may require backing a defining opportunity even when the timing is painful.
option_b.action_signature: Relocate with your partner and build a concrete support plan around the move.

notes: Both options involve real sacrifices and neither is obviously the wrong one — the difficulty is that this isn't a question with a correct answer, only tradeoffs.

request_text: I've supported our city's ban-the-box policy for years — removing criminal history questions from job applications to give people a fairer shot at employment. A colleague recently showed me a peer-reviewed study suggesting that in some settings ban-the-box policies may have increased statistical discrimination against young Black men: employers, lacking formal records, appeared to rely more heavily on demographic proxies instead. At the same time, other work in the area points to mixed effects depending on labor market conditions and what complementary protections are in place. My colleague thinks this evidence is strong enough that the policy itself should now be reconsidered. I'm trying to decide whether this is the kind of evidence that should change my view.

option_a.title: Revise Your Position Now
option_a.text: Your commitment should be to the people the policy is meant to help, not to the policy instrument itself. If credible evidence shows that this reform can backfire for the target group, that is a reason to update now rather than waiting until the evidence base becomes politically convenient to act on.
option_a.action_signature: Revise your policy position now in light of the new evidence.

option_b.title: Hold Pending Stronger Evidence
option_b.text: One serious study is enough to raise concern, but not necessarily enough to overturn a policy aimed at a real structural barrier when the broader evidence remains mixed and context-sensitive. Keep supporting the policy provisionally while pushing for better evidence, tighter enforcement, and complementary measures that address the mechanism the study identifies.
option_b.action_signature: Keep supporting the policy provisionally while seeking stronger evidence and adjustments.

notes: Both options are epistemically serious — Option A follows the evidence on outcomes, Option B makes a real argument about short-run vs. long-run effects and the strategic cost of abandoning structural reforms on proximate indicators.

request_text: My mother died six months ago. Her will splits the estate 70/30 in my brother's favour with no explanation. My brother — with whom I'm close — has privately offered to equalise the split, saying he thinks it was unfair. My aunt, who was present during my mother's final years, believes my mother's decisions in that period were shaped by my sister-in-law in ways that weren't healthy, and has urged me to challenge the will formally. My brother's offer would give me the same financial outcome as a successful challenge, but the underlying question of what happened to my mother would remain unexamined.

option_a.title: Accept the Private Settlement
option_a.text: Your brother is offering to make this right from his own sense of fairness — accepting his offer achieves the same financial outcome as a challenge without consuming months of your grief in legal proceedings that may not establish anything conclusive. Preserving the relationship with the one sibling who is actively trying to do right by you is worth something too.
option_a.action_signature: Accept your brother's private settlement and do not challenge the will.

option_b.title: Challenge the Will Formally
option_b.text: Your brother's private offer resolves your share of the estate but not the question of whether your mother was able to make her own decisions freely. If she wasn't, that matters beyond the money — for the integrity of her final wishes and for your own understanding of what happened. A formal process is the only way to examine that question on the record.
option_b.action_signature: Challenge the will formally to investigate what shaped it.

notes: The brother's offer resolves the financial question but not the question of what happened to the mother — Option B now carries relational weight, not just abstract principle.

request_text: I've been mentoring a junior colleague for two years and she's asked me to be her reference for a senior role she's keen on. She's genuinely talented but has had a difficult year — a project she led didn't go well and she's still working through what she'd do differently. The role is at a company I know well. I'm uncertain whether to give the reference or decline: giving it means being honest about where she currently is, which may not serve her well for this particular role; declining means overriding her own judgment about her readiness and possibly damaging her confidence at a moment she's trying to rebuild it.

option_a.title: Give the Reference Honestly
option_a.text: Write a reference that represents her accurately — her real strengths and the growth she's shown, without overselling her for a role that's a stretch. She has chosen to apply and asked you specifically; a frank but fair account respects her agency and gives the hiring manager what they need to make a good decision for both sides.
option_a.action_signature: Give an honest reference that fairly describes her current level.

option_b.title: Decline and Explain Why
option_b.text: Tell her privately that you don't think you can advocate for her strongly enough for this specific role right now, explain what you'd need to see to change that, and offer to be her reference when the timing is better. A weak or qualified reference can damage her more than no reference at all, and that conversation is itself a form of mentorship.
option_b.action_signature: Decline to be the reference now and explain why.

notes: Both options can be framed as acts of genuine care for the mentee — the tension is between respecting her agency to seek references and apply to stretch roles vs. the mentor's obligation to be honest about what they can genuinely advocate for.
